title: ONGC Geophysics — Full-length Mock 03
pattern: ONGC CBT — 85 MCQ · 120 min · 1 mark each · no negative marking

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Gutenberg discontinuity marks the boundary between:
questions.solution: The Gutenberg discontinuity (G-discontinuity) at ~2,900 km depth separates the solid silicate mantle from the liquid iron outer core. P-waves are strongly refracted and S-waves disappear at this boundary because the outer core is liquid.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Which type of plate boundary is characterized by the subduction of one plate beneath another?
questions.solution: At convergent boundaries, two plates move toward each other. When oceanic lithosphere meets continental lithosphere, the denser oceanic plate subducts, forming deep ocean trenches, volcanic arcs, and Wadati-Benioff zones of intermediate to deep earthquakes.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Vine–Mathews–Morley hypothesis (1963) explained the origin of:
options: Symmetric magnetic anomaly stripes on the seafloor
questions.solution: The Vine–Mathews hypothesis demonstrated that symmetric patterns of normal and reversed magnetic polarity stripes on either side of mid-ocean ridges result from seafloor spreading and geomagnetic field reversals, providing key evidence for plate tectonics.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The lithosphere–asthenosphere boundary (LAB) is primarily defined by:
options: A rheological transition from rigid to ductile behavior
A sharp drop in seismic velocity
questions.solution: The LAB is a rheological boundary where the rigid lithosphere gives way to the ductile, partially molten asthenosphere. It corresponds roughly to the ~1300°C isotherm and is detected as a low-velocity zone (LVZ) in seismic studies.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: According to the Airy model of isostasy, mountain ranges are compensated by:
options: Lateral density variations at constant depth
Deep crustal roots of lower-density material extending into the mantle
questions.solution: Airy's hypothesis (1755) proposes that topographic highs are compensated by thickened crustal 'roots' extending into the denser mantle (like icebergs). Higher mountains have deeper roots, explaining the inverse relationship between elevation and Moho depth.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The geothermal gradient in stable continental cratons is typically:
questions.solution: Stable continental cratons have low geothermal gradients (~10–15°C/km) due to thick, cold lithospheric roots. Average continental gradient is ~25–30°C/km, while tectonically active/volcanic regions can exceed 50°C/km.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: P-waves in seismology are called 'primary' waves because they:
options: Are generated at the primary focus
Travel only through the primary crust
questions.solution: P-waves (Primary/compressional) are the fastest seismic body waves and arrive first at any seismograph station. They propagate by volumetric compression and rarefaction and can travel through solids, liquids, and gases.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The moment magnitude (Mw) scale is related to earthquake source parameters through:
questions.solution: Mw = (2/3) log₁₀(M₀) − 10.7, where M₀ = μAD is the seismic moment (μ = rigidity, A = fault area, D = average displacement). Mw does not saturate for large earthquakes, unlike the Richter local magnitude ML.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: A strike-slip focal mechanism (beach ball) on a lower-hemisphere projection shows:
options: Dark quadrants at top and bottom with a vertical nodal plane
A white center surrounded by dark quadrants
Two vertical nodal planes dividing the sphere into four equal quadrants
A completely dark or white sphere
questions.solution: Strike-slip fault mechanisms have both nodal planes nearly vertical, producing four equal quadrants on the focal sphere. The P and T axes are horizontal, and the B-axis is vertical. Left-lateral and right-lateral solutions are mirror images.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Peak Ground Acceleration (PGA) is an important parameter in seismic hazard assessment because:
options: It directly measures the energy released by the earthquake
It quantifies the maximum shaking intensity relevant to structural design
It predicts the exact location of future earthquakes
questions.solution: PGA is the maximum horizontal acceleration experienced by the ground during earthquake shaking. It is the primary input for building codes and structural engineering design, directly related to the inertial forces that structures must withstand.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Conrad discontinuity, observed in some continental regions, separates:
questions.solution: The Conrad discontinuity (~15–20 km depth) separates the felsic (granitic/sialic) upper crust from the mafic (basaltic/simatic) lower crust, marked by a seismic velocity increase from ~6.0 to ~6.5 km/s. It is not universally observed and is best developed in Precambrian shields.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The quality factor Q in seismology is inversely related to:
questions.solution: Q = 2π × (energy stored per cycle / energy lost per cycle). High Q means low attenuation (waves propagate efficiently). Low Q indicates high attenuation, common in partially molten zones, sedimentary basins, and fault-damaged regions.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Bouguer correction accounts for the gravitational effect of:
options: Topographic terrain above and below the station
The slab of rock between the station and the datum elevation
Lateral density variations in the crust
Tidal variations in the gravitational field
questions.solution: The Bouguer slab correction (2πGρh) removes the gravitational attraction of the infinite slab of rock between the station and the reference datum. Combined with terrain correction, it yields the Bouguer anomaly used for subsurface density interpretation.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Reduction to the pole (RTP) in magnetic data processing is used to:
options: Center magnetic anomalies directly over their causative sources
Convert magnetic data to equivalent gravity values
questions.solution: RTP recalculates magnetic anomalies as if the inducing field were vertical (as at the magnetic pole). This removes the asymmetry caused by non-vertical field inclination, centering anomalies over their sources and simplifying geological interpretation.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: When remanent magnetization dominates (Q ratio > 1) in a rock body, the observed magnetic anomaly:
options: Is always aligned with the present-day field direction
May be offset or reversed relative to the expected anomaly from induced magnetization alone
Can be ignored for interpretation purposes
Is always weaker than the induced component
questions.solution: When Q = Mr/Mi > 1, the remanent magnetization direction (locked in at the time of rock formation) may differ significantly from the present field. This causes anomalies that are shifted, inverted, or asymmetrical compared to predictions based solely on induced magnetization.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Euler deconvolution in gravity/magnetic interpretation provides estimates of:
options: The density or magnetization contrast of the source
Source depth and structural index (geometry type)
questions.solution: Euler deconvolution uses the homogeneity equation (r · ∇T = −NT, where N is the structural index) to jointly estimate source position and geometry. Structural index N indicates source type: sphere (N=3), cylinder (N=2), dike/step (N=1), contact (N=0).

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: A negative Bouguer anomaly over a sedimentary basin typically indicates:
options: Dense basement rocks at shallow depth
Thick accumulation of low-density sediments filling a graben
questions.solution: Negative Bouguer anomalies reflect mass deficiency. Over basins, thick low-density sedimentary fill (ρ ≈ 2.0–2.4 g/cm³) relative to the denser surrounding basement (ρ ≈ 2.67–2.75 g/cm³) produces a gravity low proportional to basin depth.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Koenigsberger ratio (Q) is defined as the ratio of:
options: Total magnetic field intensity to its horizontal component
questions.solution: Q = Mr/Mi, where Mr is remanent magnetization and Mi = κH is induced magnetization (κ = susceptibility, H = ambient field). When Q > 1, remanence dominates and anomalies may not align with the present field — critical for interpreting ancient rocks and paleomagnetic data.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In the Wenner electrode configuration, the spacing between all adjacent electrodes is:
questions.solution: The Wenner array uses equal spacing 'a' between all four electrodes (A-M-N-B equally spaced). This simplifies field setup but requires more power. The geometric factor K = 2πa, and apparent resistivity ρa = 2πa(ΔV/I).

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The skin depth in magnetotelluric (MT) soundings is given by:
questions.solution: Skin depth δ ≈ 503√(ρ/f) metres, where ρ is resistivity (Ω·m) and f is frequency (Hz). Lower frequencies penetrate deeper and higher resistivity increases penetration depth. This governs the depth of investigation in MT surveys.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The 'dead band' in natural magnetotelluric signals refers to the frequency range where:
options: The electric field is too weak to measure
Natural electromagnetic signals (sferics) are unreliable and highly variable
Cultural noise completely masks the signal
questions.solution: The dead band (~1–5 kHz) lies between the Schumann resonances (ELF) and the sferic signals (VLF). Natural MT signals in this range are weak and variable, which is why controlled-source AMT (CSAMT) or radioMT methods use artificial transmitters for reliable data.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In time-domain induced polarization (IP), the chargeability is measured as:
options: The peak voltage during current flow
The integrated decay voltage normalized by the primary voltage
The ratio of secondary to primary resistance
questions.solution: Chargeability M = (1/Vp) ∫V(t)dt over a time window after current shut-off, measured in ms or mV/V. High chargeability indicates metallic mineralization (electrode polarization) or clay-rich zones (membrane polarization).

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Self-potential (SP) anomalies over massive sulfide ore bodies are typically:
questions.solution: SP anomalies over massive sulfides are characteristically negative (−100 to −500 mV) due to electrochemical (oxidation-reduction) processes at the ore–wallrock interface. The sulfide body acts as an electronic conductor connecting oxidizing and reducing zones.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The primary purpose of seismic deconvolution in data processing is to:
options: Compress the source wavelet and improve temporal resolution
Migrate dipping reflectors to their true positions
questions.solution: Deconvolution designs an inverse filter to compress the source wavelet (ideally to a spike), removing reverberations, ghosts, and short-period multiples, thereby improving temporal resolution and revealing finer subsurface stratigraphic detail.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Velocity analysis in seismic reflection processing provides velocities needed for:
options: NMO correction, stacking, and time-to-depth conversion
questions.solution: Velocity analysis (using semblance, constant-velocity stacks, or semblance panels) provides NMO velocities for moveout correction, stacking velocities for CMP summation, and interval velocities for time-to-depth conversion — essential for all subsequent processing steps.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The CMP stack improves signal-to-noise ratio by approximately:
options: n times (where n = fold of coverage)
questions.solution: For random (incoherent) noise, stacking n traces improves S/N by √n through statistical averaging. For coherent noise that stacks out (with proper NMO/DMO), improvement can be greater. This is the fundamental advantage of multi-fold CMP acquisition.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Pre-stack depth migration (PSDM) is essential when:
options: The subsurface has simple flat-layered geology
Strong lateral velocity variations exist (e.g., salt bodies, thrust belts)
Only shallow targets are being imaged
The fold of coverage is very high
questions.solution: PSDM handles strong lateral velocity variations (salt diapirs, overthrust structures) by migrating each trace before stacking using a detailed velocity model. Time migration assumes mild lateral velocity gradients and produces mispositioned events in complex geology.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Seismic refraction surveys cannot detect a low-velocity layer (LVL) beneath a high-velocity layer because:
options: The seismic source energy is insufficient
No critically refracted head wave is generated at the top of the LVL
S-waves are preferentially attenuated in the LVL
The refracted wave arrives before the direct wave
questions.solution: In an LVL, Snell's law prevents critical refraction because the required incidence angle exceeds 90° (θc = sin⁻¹(V1/V2) is undefined when V2 < V1). This 'hidden layer' problem is a fundamental limitation of the refraction method.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The maximum source-receiver offset in a seismic survey is chosen primarily based on:
options: The depth of the deepest target and the velocity structure
The number of recording channels available
questions.solution: Maximum offset must be sufficient to record reflections from the deepest target with adequate moveout for velocity analysis. A common rule of thumb is: max offset ≈ 1.5–2.0 × target depth, depending on velocity gradient and desired velocity resolution.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The natural gamma ray log in a borehole primarily distinguishes between:
options: Shale (high GR) and sandstone/limestone (low GR) lithologies
questions.solution: Gamma ray logs measure natural radioactivity from K-40, U, and Th. Shales have high GR (>75 API) due to radioactive elements in clay minerals; clean sandstones and limestones have low GR (<50 API). It is the primary lithology indicator and correlation tool.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Archie's equation relates formation resistivity to porosity and water saturation through parameters that include:
options: Tortuosity factor (a), cementation exponent (m), and saturation exponent (n)
Acoustic travel time and lithology factor
questions.solution: Archie's law: Rt = a·Rw/(φᵐ·Swⁿ). The parameters are: a (tortuosity, ~0.62–1.0), m (cementation, ~1.8–2.2), n (saturation, ~2.0), Rw (formation water resistivity). Essential for computing porosity and water saturation from resistivity logs.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: A gas-bearing zone is identified on a density–neutron porosity plot by:
options: Neutron porosity reading much higher than density porosity
Density porosity reading much higher than neutron porosity (crossover effect)
Both logs showing minimum porosity values
questions.solution: Gas has low hydrogen index (low neutron porosity) and low bulk density (high density porosity on limestone scale). The 'crossover' — density porosity > neutron porosity — is a classic gas indicator. The separation increases with gas saturation and porosity.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Wyllie time-average equation relates sonic transit time to porosity as:
questions.solution: Wyllie equation: Δt = φ·Δtf + (1−φ)·Δtma, where Δtf is fluid transit time (~189 μs/ft for water) and Δtma is matrix transit time (e.g., 55.5 μs/ft for sandstone, 47.6 μs/ft for limestone). A compaction factor (Cp) correction is needed for unconsolidated formations.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The cement bond log (CBL) evaluates well integrity by measuring:
options: The amplitude of the first-arrival E1 casing signal to assess cement quality
Natural gamma radiation from the cement sheath
Acoustic velocity through the formation behind casing
questions.solution: CBL measures E1 casing amplitude: good cement bond attenuates casing signal (low amplitude); poor bond allows strong casing resonance (high amplitude). The variable density log (VDL) provides full waveform display for comprehensive bond evaluation.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) logging provides information about:
options: Porosity, pore-size distribution, and fluid types through hydrogen proton relaxation
Formation water salinity via chlorine activation
Rock mechanical properties from acoustic measurements
questions.solution: NMR tools measure T₂ relaxation time distribution of hydrogen protons. This yields total porosity (independent of matrix), free fluid index (BVI/FFI), pore-size distribution, and can distinguish bound water from movable fluids — critical for permeability estimation.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Nyquist frequency in digital signal processing equals:
options: The highest frequency in the signal
The dominant frequency of the source wavelet
questions.solution: The Nyquist frequency = fs/2, where fs is the sampling rate. Frequencies above Nyquist alias (fold back into the signal band). Anti-alias filters must remove energy above Nyquist before digital sampling to prevent aliasing artifacts.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: A bandpass filter in seismic processing is designed to:
options: Retain the signal bandwidth while rejecting noise outside it
Convert time-domain traces to frequency domain
questions.solution: Bandpass filtering retains frequencies within a specified passband (e.g., 8–80 Hz) and attenuates frequencies outside it. This enhances S/N by removing low-frequency ground roll, high-frequency ambient noise, and out-of-band cultural noise.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Seismic inversion converts reflection seismic data into:
options: A model of acoustic impedance (velocity × density) as a function of depth
questions.solution: Seismic inversion transforms interface-property (reflectivity) data into layer-property (impedance) models. Post-stack inversion yields acoustic impedance (AI = ρVp); pre-stack AVO inversion can additionally yield S-impedance and density for lithology and fluid prediction.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In geophysical inverse theory, the resolution matrix R ideally equals the identity matrix when:
options: Each model parameter is independently resolved without trade-offs
The number of data equals the number of parameters
questions.solution: The resolution matrix R = (GTG)⁻¹GTG (linear case) maps true to estimated parameters: m_est = R·m_true. R = I means perfect resolution (each parameter recovered independently). When R ≠ I, parameters are correlated and the model is under-resolved — a fundamental limitation of non-unique inverse problems.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Seismic tomography images subsurface velocity structure by:
options: Inverting traveltime residuals from many crossing ray paths through the study volume
Measuring amplitude variations along a single profile
questions.solution: Seismic tomography uses traveltimes from many source–receiver pairs sampling a 2D/3D volume. Traveltime residuals (observed − predicted) are inverted using ray tracing and iterative linearized inversion to yield velocity perturbation models.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: What is the remainder when 2⁸ is divided by 7?
questions.solution: 2⁸ = 256. 256 ÷ 7 = 36 remainder 4. Alternatively, modular arithmetic: 2³ = 8 ≡ 1 (mod 7), so 2⁸ = (2³)²·2² ≡ 1²·4 = 4 (mod 7).

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A product costs ₹800 after a 20% discount. What was the original price?
questions.solution: Discounted price = 80% of original. 0.8 × Original = 800. Original = 800/0.8 = ₹1000.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A shopkeeper marks a product 40% above cost price and then gives a 20% discount. What is his profit percentage?

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A train 120 m long passes a pole in 12 seconds. How long will it take to cross a platform 180 m long?
questions.solution: Speed = 120/12 = 10 m/s. Total distance = 120 + 180 = 300 m. Time = 300/10 = 30 seconds.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Statements: All dogs are animals. Some animals are cats. Conclusions: (I) Some dogs are cats. (II) All cats are dogs. Which conclusion follows?
questions.solution: From the Venn diagram: 'All dogs are animals' and 'some animals are cats' does not guarantee any overlap between dogs and cats. The common animals could be non-dog animals. Neither conclusion is logically certain.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: What is the simple interest on ₹5000 at 8% per annum for 3 years?

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A can do a piece of work in 15 days and B can do it in 20 days. Working together, how many days will they take?
questions.solution: A's rate = 1/15 per day. B's rate = 1/20 per day. Combined = 1/15 + 1/20 = 4/60 + 3/60 = 7/60 per day. Time = 60/7 = 8⁴⁄₇ days ≈ 8.57 days.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: If A:B = 3:5 and B:C = 2:3, what is A:B:C?
questions.solution: A:B = 3:5, B:C = 2:3. Make B common: A:B = 6:10, B:C = 10:15. Therefore A:B:C = 6:10:15.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Two dice are thrown simultaneously. What is the probability that the sum is 7?
questions.solution: Favorable outcomes: (1,6),(2,5),(3,4),(4,3),(5,2),(6,1) = 6 outcomes. Total outcomes = 36. Probability = 6/36 = 1/6.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: If DELHI is coded as CDKGH, how is MUMBAI coded?
questions.solution: Each letter is shifted by −1 in the alphabet: D→C, E→D, L→K, H→G, I→H. Applying −1 to MUMBAI: M→L, U→T, M→L, B→A, A→Z, I→H → LTLAZH.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: If x + 1/x = 5, what is x² + 1/x²?

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: The diagonal of a square is 10√2 cm. What is its area?
questions.solution: Diagonal d = a√2, where a is the side. 10√2 = a√2 → a = 10 cm. Area = a² = 100 cm².

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Find the next number: 2, 6, 12, 20, 30, ?

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: The average of 5 numbers is 20. If one number is removed, the average becomes 18. What is the removed number?
questions.solution: Sum of 5 numbers = 5 × 20 = 100. Sum of remaining 4 = 4 × 18 = 72. Removed number = 100 − 72 = 28.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Pipe A can fill a tank in 10 hours and Pipe B can empty it in 15 hours. If both are opened simultaneously, how long will it take to fill the tank?
questions.solution: A's filling rate = 1/10 per hour. B's emptying rate = 1/15 per hour. Net rate = 1/10 − 1/15 = 3/30 − 2/30 = 1/30 per hour. Time = 30 hours.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Pointing to a woman, Raj said, 'She is the mother of my father's only grandchild.' How is the woman related to Raj?
questions.solution: Raj's father's only grandchild = Raj's child (Raj being the only child). The mother of Raj's child = Raj's wife.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: What is the volume of a cube with side length 5 cm?
questions.solution: Volume of cube = side³ = 5³ = 125 cm³.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: What is the HCF of 24, 36, and 60?

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A man walks 5 km North, turns right and walks 3 km, turns right again and walks 5 km. How far and in which direction is he from the start?
questions.solution: Path: (0,0) → 5 km N → (0,5) → 3 km E → (3,5) → 5 km S → (3,0). He is 3 km East of the starting point.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A boat travels 20 km upstream in 5 hours and 20 km downstream in 2 hours. What is the speed of the boat in still water?
questions.solution: Upstream speed = 20/5 = 4 km/h. Downstream speed = 20/2 = 10 km/h. Speed of boat = (10 + 4)/2 = 7 km/h. Speed of current = (10 − 4)/2 = 3 km/h.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: The angle of elevation of the top of a building from a point 50 m away is 30°. What is the height of the building?

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Doctor : Hospital :: Teacher : ?
questions.solution: A doctor works in a hospital; a teacher works in a school. The relationship is professional : workplace.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: If January 1, 2024 was a Monday, what day of the week was January 1, 2025?
questions.solution: 2024 is a leap year (366 days). 366 mod 7 = 2. Monday + 2 days = Wednesday.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: In what ratio must rice at ₹30/kg be mixed with rice at ₹40/kg to produce a mixture worth ₹34/kg?
questions.solution: Using alligation: (40 − 34):(34 − 30) = 6:4 = 3:2. So ₹30 rice : ₹40 rice = 3:2.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Which number is the odd one out: 4, 9, 16, 23, 36, 49?
questions.solution: 4=2², 9=3², 16=4², 36=6², 49=7² — all perfect squares. 23 is not a perfect square, making it the odd one out.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: ONGC was established in which year?
questions.solution: Oil and Natural Gas Corporation (ONGC) was established on August 14, 1956, as a government company under the Indian Companies Act. It was later converted to a public limited company in 1994.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: The President of India is elected by:
options: An electoral college of elected members of Parliament and state legislatures
questions.solution: Article 54 provides that the President is elected by an electoral college comprising elected members of both Houses of Parliament and the elected members of the Legislative Assemblies of states and union territories of Delhi and Puducherry.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: The Bombay High (Mumbai High) offshore oil field is located in which basin?
questions.solution: Mumbai High (formerly Bombay High) is India's largest offshore oil field, located about 160 km west of Mumbai in the Mumbai Offshore Basin. Discovered in 1974, it produces from Eocene carbonates.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: India's first deep-water gas discovery in the Krishna-Godavari basin was made by:
questions.solution: Reliance Industries made the major D-6 (KG-D6) discovery in the Krishna-Godavari deep-water basin in 2002, one of India's largest gas discoveries with estimated reserves of about 14 TCF.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: The Paris Agreement on climate change aims to limit global warming to:
questions.solution: The Paris Agreement (2015) aims to limit warming to well below 2°C above pre-industrial levels, with efforts to limit it to 1.5°C, recognizing that this would significantly reduce the risks of climate change.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: The Directorate General of Hydrocarbons (DGH) in India is responsible for:
options: Overseeing upstream exploration and production activities
questions.solution: DGH is the technical arm of the Ministry of Petroleum and Natural Gas, responsible for regulating and promoting upstream (exploration and production) hydrocarbon activities under the NELP and HELP frameworks.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: Which gas is commonly used in LPG cylinders for domestic cooking in India?
questions.solution: LPG (Liquefied Petroleum Gas) used in Indian households is primarily a mixture of propane (C₃H₈) and butane (C₄H₁₀), liquefied under moderate pressure for convenient storage and transport.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: India's strategic petroleum reserves are located at:
questions.solution: India's underground strategic petroleum reserves (SPR) are carved out of rock caverns at Visakhapatnam (1.33 MMT), Mangalore (1.5 MMT), and Padur (2.5 MMT) for emergency oil supply.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: India's first offshore oil well was drilled at:
options: Aliabet in the Gulf of Cambay
questions.solution: India's first offshore well was drilled at Aliabet in the Gulf of Cambay (Khambhat) in 1962, predating the discovery of Bombay High (1974) by over a decade.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: The New Exploration Licensing Policy (NELP), introduced in India in 1997, was designed to:
options: Open the upstream E&P sector to private and foreign participation through competitive bidding
Ban foreign investment in the energy sector
Restrict exploration to ONGC and Oil India only
questions.solution: NELP (1997) replaced the earlier licensing regime with competitive international bidding, allowing private Indian and foreign companies to explore for hydrocarbons. It aimed to attract investment, technology, and expertise to India's exploration sector.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Choose the word most similar in meaning to 'ABUNDANT':
questions.solution: Abundant means existing in large quantities; plentiful. It is the antonym of scarce or limited.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Choose the word most opposite in meaning to 'BENIGN':
questions.solution: Benign means gentle, kindly, or not harmful. Malignant means harmful, hostile, or cancerous — the direct opposite.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Identify the grammatically correct sentence:
options: The group of scientists were conducting experiments.
Neither the manager nor the employees was available.
Each of the candidates has submitted their application.
The news are not good today.
questions.solution: 'Each' is a singular indefinite pronoun requiring the singular verb 'has'. Errors: (A) 'group' (collective noun) takes 'was'; (B) verb should agree with nearest subject 'employees' → 'were'; (D) 'news' is uncountable → 'is'.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: What does the idiom 'to burn the midnight oil' mean?
options: To work late into the night
questions.solution: 'Burn the midnight oil' means to work or study late into the night, originally referring to working by oil lamp light before the advent of electricity.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Read the passage: 'The monsoon is a seasonal wind pattern that brings heavy rainfall to the Indian subcontinent. It is driven by the differential heating of land and sea.' What primarily drives the monsoon?
options: Differential heating of land and sea
questions.solution: The passage explicitly states the monsoon is driven by differential heating of land and sea, which creates pressure gradients that reverse wind direction seasonally.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: A person who loves books is called a:
questions.solution: Bibliophile = book lover (biblio = book, philia = love). Philatelist collects stamps; Numismatist collects coins; Misanthropist dislikes humanity.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Choose the correct preposition: 'He is proficient ___ English and French.'
questions.solution: 'Proficient in' is the correct collocation. We say 'proficient in a language' or 'proficient at a skill'.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Choose the correctly spelt word:
questions.solution: The correct spelling is 'Accommodation' — double 'c' and double 'm'. This is one of the most commonly misspelt words in English.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Choose the word most opposite in meaning to 'TRANSPARENT':
questions.solution: Transparent means allowing light to pass through clearly. Opaque means not allowing light to pass through — the direct opposite.

questions.subject: English Language
questions.stem: Choose the correct form: 'If I had known about the earthquake warning, I __________ the building earlier.'
questions.solution: Third conditional (past unreal condition): If + past perfect, would have + past participle. 'If I had known... I would have left' describes an unreal past situation and its hypothetical consequence.
